home.title: SweFOR Maghreb Network
home.subtitle: Building Peace Through Dialogue and Nonviolence
home.description: Since 2018, the SweFOR Maghreb Network has been fostering cross-border youth dialogue and peacebuilding across Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, and Western Sahara. With over 7 years of sustained engagement, we've created spaces for dialogue, training, and mutual understanding among young peace advocates.

timeline.title: Project Timeline
timeline.subtitle: From 2018 to 2026: A Journey of Peace and Dialogue

projects.title: Active Projects
projects.subtitle: Initiatives Advancing Peacebuilding in the Maghreb

projects.name: Maghreb Network
projects.description: Cross-border youth dialogue bringing together young peace advocates from Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, and Western Sahara for sustained peacebuilding engagement.

projects.name: Training of Trainers
projects.description: Facilitator development program equipping youth with skills in nonviolence, dialogue facilitation, and peacebuilding methodologies.

projects.name: Dialogue Workshops
projects.description: Series of workshops focused on communication skills, conflict transformation, and building mutual understanding across borders.

projects.name: Climate & Conflict
projects.description: Exploring the nexus between environmental sustainability and peacebuilding, engaging youth on climate adaptation and dialogue.

projects.name: Network Meetings
projects.description: Monthly online coordination meetings maintaining connection, sharing updates, and planning collaborative peacebuilding activities.

projects.buildingOnSuccessDesc: These five projects form the foundation of our work. As we move forward, we're designing new initiatives focused on climate-peace integration, cross-border dialogue, and formalizing youth leadership development.

vision.title: Strategic Vision
vision.subtitle: Building on 7+ Years of Peacebuilding Excellence
vision.context: Drawing on nearly a decade of experience in cross-border youth dialogue and nonviolent peacebuilding across the Maghreb region, we propose three complementary strategic initiatives to scale impact and ensure sustained engagement for peace.

proposals.title: Maghreb Climate-Peace Youth Initiative
proposals.overview: Connect climate adaptation with peacebuilding by engaging youth from Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, and Western Sahara around shared environmental challenges.
proposals.rationale: 44% of the Maghreb population is youth. Water scarcity and environmental change affect all countries equally, providing natural dialogue entry points across political divides.
activities: Local climate-peace workshops in each country
Cross-border youth exchanges and study tours
Training of Trainers program for climate-peace facilitators
Online platform for sustained dialogue and knowledge-sharing
proposals.alignment: Aligned with SweFOR's nonviolence focus and IFOR's youth empowerment mandate

proposals.title: Cross-Border Dialogue for Maghreb Unity
proposals.overview: Address democratic deficit and Morocco-Algeria tensions through structured people-to-people dialogue, focusing on Western Sahara as a dialogue topic.
proposals.rationale: Youth-led dialogue circles can build understanding without imposing political positions. Network of 20+ trained facilitators ready to scale engagement.
activities: Youth-led dialogue circles in each country
Regional youth forums on unity and cooperation
proposals.alignment: Aligned with UNDP Maghreb Strategy 2025-2030 and UN Sustainable Development Goals

proposals.title: Nonviolent Futures: Maghreb Youth Leadership Academy
proposals.overview: Formalize the Training of Trainers program into a certified academy, creating sustained impact through a 3-year leadership cycle.
proposals.rationale: Move from ad-hoc training to structured certification. Build alumni network for long-term influence. Integrate nonviolence methodology deeply.
activities: Year 1: Foundation - Core nonviolence principles and facilitation basics
Year 2: Specialization - Climate, dialogue, or conflict transformation tracks
Year 3: Mentoring - Advanced facilitation and alumni leadership roles
proposals.alignment: Aligned with IFOR's nonviolence education and SweFOR's peacebuilding excellence

vision.nextSteps: Erik Nilsson and Nabil Bahhar will meet in Rome on April 20-21, 2026 to evaluate the 2018-2026 period and finalize strategic direction.
vision.smartDescription: Every objective, every milestone, every deliverable is designed to be tracked and verified.
vision.summaryText: Building on 2018–2026 experience, these projects represent the next chapter of SweFOR Maghreb Network engagement — inclusive, measurable, and designed for lasting impact across every country in the region.

footer.tagline: Building Peace Through Dialogue and Nonviolence

tools.title: Peacebuilding Tools
tools.subtitle: A quick refresher before you design your project ideas
tools.intro: Before jumping into project design, take a moment to ground your thinking. These four tools work together: analyze the conflict (3-Box), understand its trajectory (Curle), position your strategy (RPP), then map your logic of change (ToC). They are not bureaucratic checklists — they protect your project from common blind spots.

threeBox.title: The 3-Box Model
threeBox.subtitle: Sort what you know about the conflict
threeBox.description: A simple sorting frame to make sense of complex conflict information. Lay every fact you have about the situation into one of three columns. The goal is clarity — not perfection.

box1.title: Forces FOR Peace / Against Conflict
box1.q1: What forces exist NOW that we can build on?
box1.q2: Where do people already cooperate across conflict lines?

box2.title: Key Actors
box2.q1: Who can strongly influence the conflict — positively or negatively?
box2.q2: Note: not necessarily program participants — these are decision-makers.

box3.title: Forces FOR Conflict / Against Peace
box3.q1: What dynamics push people toward violence or division?
box3.q2: Which issues divide people, and how?

threeBox.use: You are starting a new analysis and need to organize raw information before identifying the Key Driving Factors (KDFs) of the conflict.

curle.title: The Curle Diagram
curle.subtitle: Where is the conflict on its transformation curve?
curle.description: Adam Curle's model maps the journey from latent conflict to sustainable peace. Each phase needs a different kind of intervention — what works in phase 1 fails in phase 4, and vice versa.

phase1.title: Latent Conflict
phase1.desc: Raise awareness, organize community, build coalitions, conscientization.

phase2.title: Overt Conflict
phase2.desc: Nonviolent action to balance power: monitoring, advocacy, peacekeeping.

phase3.title: Conflict Settlement
phase3.desc: Negotiation, mediation, persuasion to convert or accommodate interests.

phase4.title: Sustainable Peace & Justice
phase4.desc: Principled negotiation that addresses the main interests of all groups.

curle.use: You need to choose the RIGHT type of intervention for the current phase. A dialogue program in latent conflict misses the mark — you need consciousness-raising first.

rpp.title: RPP Matrix
rpp.subtitle: Reflecting on Peace Practice — position your strategy
rpp.description: A 2×2 matrix asking two questions about your project: WHO are you working with, and WHAT KIND of change are you seeking? Most projects sit in just one cell — the lesson from RPP is that this is rarely enough.

cell1.label: More × Individual
cell1.example: Mass dialogue, youth training, awareness campaigns reaching wide audiences.

cell2.label: Key × Individual
cell2.example: Leadership coaching, working with influential individuals to shift their views.

cell3.label: More × Socio-Political
cell3.example: Mobilization, civic movements, popular pressure for systemic reform.

cell4.label: Key × Socio-Political
cell4.example: Policy advocacy, institutional reform, peace agreements with elites.

rpp.key1: Work in just ONE cell rarely creates lasting change — you need bridges between cells.
rpp.key2: Individual-level work alone doesn't translate into political/structural change.
rpp.key3: Engaging only key people without broader support — or only the population without leaders — both fail.
rpp.use: You have a project idea and want to test whether it will actually scale into structural change. Map your activities on the matrix — where are the gaps?

toc.title: Theory of Change
toc.subtitle: From your activities to the change you want to see
toc.description: A Theory of Change is the explicit logic linking what you DO to the change you want to SEE. It surfaces the assumptions hiding between activities and impact — the places where projects most often fail.

step1.label: Inputs
step1.desc: Resources, funding, partners, expertise you bring.

step2.label: Activities
step2.desc: Workshops, dialogues, trainings, advocacy you run.

step3.label: Outputs
step3.desc: Direct, countable results: # trained, # events held.

step4.label: Outcomes
step4.desc: Behavior, attitude, capacity changes in participants.

toc.questionsTitle: Critical questions to stress-test your ToC
toc.q1: WHY do we believe that doing X will lead to Y? What is the assumption?
toc.q2: What evidence do we have that this causal chain works?
toc.q3: What could break the chain — and how do we mitigate it?
toc.q4: Are our outcomes truly within our power to influence — or are we promising what we can't deliver?
toc.use: You are designing or pitching a project. A clear ToC makes assumptions visible, helps donors trust the logic, and gives you a roadmap for measuring whether it actually works.

tips.title: Quick Tips Before You Start
tips.subtitle: Common pitfalls to avoid when designing your project
tips.t1: Don't confuse conflict analysis with context analysis — focus on dynamics, not background.
tips.t2: There is no single objective truth about a conflict. Your analysis is shaped by who is in the room.
tips.t3: Update your analysis regularly. Conflicts evolve — your tools should too.
tips.t4: Identify factors as quantities (level of, amount of) — not as actors. Actors aren't factors; their behavior is.
tips.t5: Test every Key Driving Factor: if it changes, does the situation really change?
tips.t6: Combine tools when needed. They complement each other — no single tool gives the whole picture.

cta.title: Now — design your project
cta.text: These tools are scaffolding, not cages. Use them to surface blind spots, then trust your judgment as practitioners. The goal is a project that is clear, honest about its assumptions, and rooted in real conflict dynamics — not a project that ticks boxes.
